author: @robowolf883
text:
Awesome video! I've been searching for a long time to see if anyone else has measured the hall effect outside of academic research, glad I stumbled upon this. Your change in Hall Voltage is incredible. For those of you who are interested in what exactly is happening here(I'm no expert, just read a lot on the H.E.);

What is known as the Hall Effect, is really a product of the Lorentz, or Magnetic Force. When an electrically charged particle(lets say an electron) intercepts a magnetic field(strongest at 90°), due to the motion and charge of the particle(negative), the particle will experience a sideways force perpendicular to both the magnetic field the particle crosses, and the direction the particle is travelling in(stick your right hand out and point your index forward(velocity of q), thumb up(magnetic field, B), and middle, left(resulting force, F)). If we consider that an electric current is simply the motion of charged particles(electricity) through a conductor(the Hall Element), the particles, or "charge carriers" are subject to the same forces, this is why a voltage potential shows up on the edges of the element, but this same potential also draws the deflected carriers back to the center current, where the magnetic force, and the electric force(generated by the separated charges) cancels out producing the final Hall Voltage measurement. This Hall Voltage can be calculated using the following formula:

V(H) = (Ix * Bz) / (charge carrier density, n) * (thickness of element, t) * (charge of electron, e). 

But I'm not finished yet (Inhales deeply); The reason I find the result in the video surprising is(for I will also give a conceptual understanding of the above formula): If one considers again an electric current possessed by the Hall element, lets say in the x direction; *but through what material?*, it's known that the choice of conductor matters(i.e. resistance), but why? For this, the question is not of the magnitude of the current, but rather what is the mechanism by which material x, bears the current? e.g. "how does copper conduct 5 amps, vs silicon?" One(Si) opposes current more than Cu does, thus electricity must conduct differently in silicon than it does in copper, and indeed it does. The answer to this explains the VH(I promise). The reason Cu is a better conductor than Si is, is primarily due to two factors, The NUMBER of charge carriers(i.e. electrons and holes) able to freely move(and conduct a current), within a given unit of volume(typically in n/cm^3 for semiconductors, and n/m^3 for metals); this is charge carrier density denoted n, and *how freely* said carriers are able to move without being impeded, also known as charge carrier *mobility*, denoted μe, and μh (electrons, holes*). The difference in Cu & Si, is that Cu, has an awfully low mobility, its snails' pace, you could literally walk faster than an electron moving in a circuit. But this slowbility is made up for the fact that Cu has an *enormous* number of those carriers to conduct the current, albeit slow. In silicon its almost the other way around, there are barely any carriers available to conduct electricity, but the ones that do, are absolutely hauling it(just not fast enough to make up for it's low carrier concentration, inversely like copper). The key is to scroll back up to the magnetic force I recently described, and recall that, and I quote: "due to the *motion and charge*...", By this, I mean that the product of the velocity of the charge, and the quantity of charge(and the magnetic field strength!) determine the strength of the Lorentz Force. Ah! It makes sense, a material who's carriers move faster, are more strongly deflected by the Lorentz Force! This is true, but there's one more caveat; one can see that the *thickness* of the element is inversely associated with the measured voltage; Finally, remember, when we look at current through a conductor, its charge carrier density contributes to its conductivity; this implies that one could calculate the number of carriers present in a conductor of known dimensions via the carrier density; also implying the carrier content is fixed for an element of said dimensions. LASTLY, this means, in a constant current situation(I is fixed, despite R), and in thicker Hall elements, there are more carriers to bear I due to a larger volume, which don't have to move as fast to bear I, which also get less deflected by B from moving slower = lower VH. BUT, in *thinner* elements, less volume means less carriers, and if I is to remain constant, these carriers must move quicker to make up for the lack of them, which is what it means for a thin element to conduct current(they ought to call this the "haul effect"). This is why thinner elements of lower carrier concentration, and high carrier mobility, are suitable as Hall elements.

Which is where this finding is interesting. The Hall Effect is usually only measurable in laboratory environments, where semiconducting crystals and atomically thin films of metal can be made to T, for the Hall Effect is a *very* weak phenomenon; this experiment was originally discovered using gold leaf, which is on the order of sub-micron thickness... Even then the VH is only measurable within millivolts. And idk about you, but I don't know anyone who's got an extra wafer of Gallium Arsenide to sell me for less than $600... 

But, Graphene, is an interesting conductor... You see, graphite, and especially it's graphene sheets aren't bad conductors at all! The confusion arises when one finds that these materials have low carrier density, on a level that one would suspect such materials of possesing a high resistivity, but when the theory of conduction is applied, we discover that graphite/graphene's decent combination of conductivity & low carrier density results in C6 possessing an absurdly high carrier mobility. (I presume this may be partly due to the π-bonds conducting electrons outside the lattice where few obstructions exists to impede the current.*) You must ponder, a single atom thick and probably the highest measured mobility of any known substance - is truly the ideal Hall element. (Again, I'm by no means an expert, this is just how I came to interpret the theory.*)

I was just on my way to constructing methods sensitive enough for a hobbyist like me to detect the HE myself, like Edwin Hall did in 1879. You've inspired me to include this material in my potential soon-to-be experiments, and given me hope that I may actually be able to measure the effect with limited resources. Thank you for sharing your results!

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
Just adding something to the discussion. Bldc motor contollers that use hall sensors know the position of a magnet within a sector, range of degrees of rotation. 

With 3 hall effect sensors and 2 poles, that means knowing the position of a magnet within 60 degrees of rotation.  With 3 hall sensors and 4 poles that's  (360/(3×4))=30 degrees.  

Then depending on which sector a magnet is in, as told by the hall sensor signals, the motor contoller energizes any two of three wires to the motor so as to torque to the motor's rotor.

But because of the degrees of freedom within a sector, and a variable load on rotor makes it's imposible to know or calculate  the exact rotation position. Eitherway, all that really maters is knowing which two of three wires to energize + and -  by knowing which sector a magnet is in.

author: @scotttovey
text:
Hmm, could you make something like a reed switch?

This would be difficult as a reed switch uses two different metals that when a magnetic field crosses them they polarize and attract each other and make contact, thereby closing the switch until the magnetic field moves away.

I've read that the metal in reed switches can become magnetized which causes the two metals be become permanently closed. Perhaps a graphene painted reed style switch would not be magnetizable? I haven't the slightest idea how one would go about making graphene painted contacts react opposite to the same magnetic field. But if possible, it would certainly be an extended life reed switch.

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
@kreynolds1123 
Thanks for the explanation.
The hall effect seems then the better solution despite the use of a current.
😎👍

author: @TheOldRecordStation
text: How sensitive can you get this? Because if you you can further this, like you said, there are mass of applications, including alternative energy. Now one thing you could use this for is instead of using a tiny sensor that can burn out on a BLDC motor make panels of these to sense the timing to send to the controller. Now there's an idea! ;)

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
Signal strength is proportional to  primary current and magnetic field strength. The greater the current through the hall sensor, the stronger it interacts with the magnetic field. 

But rather than carry more current,  hall effect signals are often amplified.

On my channel, I made an axial flux BLDC motor that doesnt use hall sensors.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that is an interesting idea mate and to be honest i have no idea right now how sensitive they can be - the next thing really is an amplifier circuit so it can be made small

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @kreynolds1123 exactly mate
